speaker: ALEX
text: Welcome back. Today we're working with one of the most important parts of GitHub: issues.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And for a lot of learners, this is where GitHub starts to feel social, right? Not just files and commits, but conversation.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Issues are where people report bugs, request features, ask for clarification, track chores, and coordinate work. In open source, a lot of collaboration begins there.

speaker: ALEX
text: Before we get into the mechanics, a few support notes matter here. This chapter pairs well with the official GitHub documentation about issues, GitHub's accessibility guide for issues, and the GitHub Accessibility Lab CLI guide. There are also related appendices on advanced search, GitHub Mobile, and a screen reader cheat sheet.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if someone wants the official source of truth, or a companion reference with current product details, those are the places to check.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. And there's an accessibility note that matters especially for screen reader users. This lesson assumes GitHub's improved issues experience, which has better landmark structure and live announcements. Depending on your account, this may already be active automatically.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How would someone verify that?

speaker: ALEX
text: Open the user menu at the top right of GitHub, go to Feature preview, and look for the New Issues Experience. If it's listed and enabled, you're set. If it's listed and not enabled, turn it on. If it's not listed at all, that usually means it has already become part of the standard interface.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And for NVDA users, there's also that constant question: browse mode or focus mode?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Use browse mode for reading headings, lists, and issue content. Switch to focus mode when typing in fields like search boxes, title boxes, and comment editors. And remember that NVDA plus F7 is a strong orientation tool because it gives you lists of headings, links, form fields, buttons, and landmarks.

speaker: ALEX
text: This chapter is also the first issue-based challenge chapter. The pattern is simple: claim, act, confirm.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Meaning first understand where you are and what you're supposed to do, then do the task, then verify the result.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. There are two core challenges and one optional extension. First, create your first issue. Second, comment on another issue and use an at-mention. Optional: add a sub-issue if that feature is enabled in your repository.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And importantly, no branch work yet.

speaker: ALEX
text: Correct. No branch, no file editing, no pull request. This chapter is all issue work. Your evidence is in the issue threads and issue metadata.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's useful because beginners often assume every GitHub action needs a branch.

speaker: ALEX
text: Not here. When you complete the challenges, you post evidence in your assigned challenge issues, including the issue numbers you created or commented on, and then you close those assigned challenge issues.

speaker: ALEX
text: If you prefer the command line, there are a few good habits to keep in sequence. First, confirm repository context with commands like git remote dash v and gh repo view. Second, read before writing: use gh issue view and understand the issue before you comment or edit. Third, keep comments focused and actionable. Fourth, verify each action from output, such as checking gh issue list dash dash assignee at me. And fifth, stay with documented options from gh help and the manual.

speaker: JAMIE
text: I like that. It keeps terminal work from becoming guesswork.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Especially for accessibility and reliability.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's ground this. What is a GitHub issue, really?

speaker: ALEX
text: A GitHub issue is a tracked conversation attached to a repository. It has a title, a body, metadata like labels and assignees, a numbered identity such as issue 150, and a timeline of comments and activity.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So it's more structured than a chat message.

speaker: ALEX
text: Much more. Issues are durable. They can be searched, filtered, referenced, assigned, grouped into milestones, linked to pull requests, and sometimes broken into sub-issues.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you get to the issues list?

speaker: ALEX
text: Two common ways. From a repository page, activate the Issues tab in the repository navigation. Or go directly by URL if you know the repository path and append slash issues.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And for keyboard users, there are shortcuts too.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. On GitHub, pressing G then I jumps to issues in many contexts. That can be faster than tabbing through repository navigation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What should a learner expect on the issues list page?

speaker: ALEX
text: Think of it as a dashboard of conversations. You'll usually find the page heading, controls for filtering and searching, buttons for open and closed issues, maybe labels or milestone filters, the New issue button, and then the list itself.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How should someone read that list with a screen reader?

speaker: ALEX
text: Jump by headings if the page is structured that way, or use links and list navigation. Each issue entry usually tells you the title, issue number, whether it's open or closed, who opened it, and sometimes labels, assignees, comments count, or related metadata.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the key skill is not just finding the list, but learning what each row is announcing.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. You're listening for enough information to decide whether to open it.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And once the list gets crowded, filtering becomes the real superpower.

speaker: ALEX
text: Absolutely. GitHub gives you a search and filter bar, plus filter controls. You can filter by label, combine filters, filter by milestone, search by keywords, and switch between open and closed issues.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can we make that more concrete?

speaker: ALEX
text: Sure. If you want beginner-friendly work, filter by the label good first issue. If you want issues assigned to you, use an assignee filter. If you want a specific planning target, filter by milestone. And if you know a word from the title or description, search with that keyword.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And combining filters means narrowing the list with more than one condition at once.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. For example: open issues with a specific label and a specific milestone. That gives you a much smaller, more relevant list.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's talk terminal. What if someone wants to list their assigned challenge issues from the command line?

speaker: ALEX
text: You can use the GitHub CLI to list issues, including filtering for issues assigned to you. A typical pattern is gh issue list with an assignee filter such as at me. The exact flags can vary, so use gh issue list dash dash help when needed.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And viewing a specific issue in the terminal?

speaker: ALEX
text: That's typically gh issue view followed by the issue number. One of the nice things there is that Markdown renders in the terminal output, so headings, lists, and emphasis are easier to read than raw source.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What if the terminal view isn't enough?

speaker: ALEX
text: You can open the issue in your browser instead, and there are options to focus on the comments if you only want to read discussion. The principle is the same: choose the view that helps you read effectively.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So now we've found issues. How do we read one well?

speaker: ALEX
text: When you land on an issue page, first orient yourself. Identify the title, the issue number, whether it's open or closed, and the main issue description. Then look at the comment thread and activity timeline.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What does quick navigation look like here?

speaker: ALEX
text: Use headings, landmarks, or element lists with your screen reader. If you're sighted or low vision, scan for the title at the top, the description body beneath it, and the activity thread below. If you're in the terminal, gh issue view can present the description and comments in a clean sequence.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And what specifically should we read in the issue description?

speaker: ALEX
text: Look for what happened, what the author expected, how to reproduce the problem if it's a bug, what environment they were using, and any extra context. Even if those exact labels aren't used, those are the core ideas.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then comments tell you how the conversation evolved.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Comments may confirm the problem, ask follow-up questions, suggest fixes, reference related issues or pull requests, or note status changes.

speaker: JAMIE
text: All right. Let's leave a comment.

speaker: ALEX
text: There are two main styles with the GitHub CLI. Interactive mode opens your default editor, usually through the EDITOR environment variable, so you can write the comment there. Inline mode lets you provide the comment text directly on the command line.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And in the browser?

speaker: ALEX
text: Open the issue, move to the comment box at the bottom, type your message, and activate Comment. On many GitHub pages, Control plus Enter submits the comment.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about Markdown while typing?

speaker: ALEX
text: GitHub comments support Markdown, so you can use plain paragraphs, lists, inline code, links, and emphasis. But for issue comments, clarity matters more than decoration. Keep it focused.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And helpful.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. A useful comment might confirm a bug, clarify reproduction steps, suggest a location in the repository, or offer a next action.

speaker: JAMIE
text: This is where at-mentions come in, right?

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Typing at followed by a username notifies that person. It's a direct way to bring someone into the conversation.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if I comment, "At classmate, I can confirm the link is broken," GitHub alerts them.

speaker: ALEX
text: That's right. And that's why the chapter's second challenge includes an at-mention. It teaches the communication pattern, not just the typing pattern.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can we walk through that challenge clearly?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Open your Learning Room repository's Issues tab. Find an issue created by another student, or a facilitator-provided practice issue. Open it and read the description first. Then go to the comment box. Write a helpful comment that includes an at-mention of the issue author. For example, you might confirm the problem, correct a shortcut, or suggest where new text should go. Then submit the comment.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And you're done when the comment appears in the thread and the username renders as a link.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now let's file a new issue.

speaker: ALEX
text: From the repository's Issues page, activate New issue. Some repositories show a template picker first. If so, choose a suitable template, or open a blank issue if that's what the challenge calls for.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Then we fill out the form.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. The most common pieces are the title and the description body. The title should be specific. Not "Problem" or "Help." Instead, something like "Keyboard shortcuts table has incorrect NVDA modifier key" or "Setup guide link to accessibility settings is broken."

speaker: JAMIE
text: So the title should help someone understand the issue before opening it.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Then in the body, explain what happened, what you expected, how to reproduce it if relevant, your environment, and any additional context.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's unpack those.

speaker: ALEX
text: What happened means the actual behavior or problem. What I expected means the correct or desired behavior. How to reproduce means the exact steps someone else can follow to observe the same issue. Environment includes things like browser, operating system, device, screen reader, zoom level, or other tools involved. Additional context is anything else that makes the report more understandable.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And labels can be assigned from the sidebar if you have permission.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Labels help classify the issue. Then you submit it.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about the CLI version of filing an issue?

speaker: ALEX
text: There are two broad approaches. Interactive mode prompts you for title, body, labels, and assignees. Inline mode lets you provide those values directly on the command line. And if the repository has issue templates, you may be able to use one of those templates rather than starting from scratch.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So browser and CLI both support structured issue creation, just with different interaction styles.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can we go through the first challenge step by step?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Open your Learning Room repository. Go to Issues, either through the tab or with G then I. Activate New issue. If a template picker appears, choose Open a blank issue unless a template clearly fits. In the title field, enter a clear title of at least about a dozen characters. In the body, write a meaningful description of at least around eighty characters. Include what the problem is, where it is in the repository, and what fix you think should happen. Then submit the issue and save the issue number or URL.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the chapter frames this as an "agentic strategy" too.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. A clear issue is not only useful for humans. It is also a strong prompt for an AI agent. If you describe an accessibility problem or content chore clearly, you make it easier for a person or a tool to act on it.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's a great bridge between accessibility, open source, and AI workflows.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about cross-referencing issues?

speaker: ALEX
text: Very important. You can mention another issue in a comment by typing its number or a full reference. In many contexts, GitHub turns that into a link.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And there are also closing keywords.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. In pull request descriptions, and sometimes in issue comments depending on context, keywords like closes, fixes, or resolves followed by an issue reference can automatically close the linked issue when the pull request is merged.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's useful because it connects discussion to implementation.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. And cross-repo references let you point to an issue in another repository when work spans projects.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now the optional extension: sub-issues.

speaker: ALEX
text: Sub-issues let you break a larger issue into smaller, trackable child issues. The parent issue can show progress as those child issues are completed.

speaker: JAMIE
text: When should someone use sub-issues?

speaker: ALEX
text: When a problem is too large to track as one step. For example, "Fix accessibility in welcome dot md" might be split into "Add alt text," "Fix heading hierarchy," and "Correct link text."

speaker: JAMIE
text: How do you create one?

speaker: ALEX
text: Open the parent issue. Look for a Sub-issues section, an Add sub-issue button, or a Create sub-issue option. Create a new sub-issue with a clear title and short description. Once created, it appears nested under the parent.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And when you're reading a parent issue, you can see its child issues there.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. And when you read a child issue, GitHub may show its parent relationship too.

speaker: JAMIE
text: How is that different from a task list?

speaker: ALEX
text: A task list is lighter weight, usually just checkboxes in issue text. Sub-issues are full issues with their own pages, metadata, discussion, and tracking. Use task lists for simple checkboxes; use sub-issues when each piece deserves independent conversation and status.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's shift into maintainer and triager skills. What does managing issues include?

speaker: ALEX
text: Closing and reopening issues, assigning them, changing labels, setting milestones, and sometimes transferring or deleting issues if you have the necessary permissions.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Closing sounds simple, but there are nuances.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. You can close an issue directly. In some workflows you may specify a reason, such as completed or not planned. You may also close with a comment so the author understands why. And if closure was premature, you can reopen the issue.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And assigning means identifying who will work on it.

speaker: ALEX
text: Correct. You might assign yourself, add or remove labels, or set a milestone to place the issue in a release or project target.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about changing labels?

speaker: ALEX
text: Labels aren't fixed forever. As understanding improves, an issue may move from question to bug, or from general to accessibility, or gain a priority label. Updating labels is part of triage.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And transferring or deleting an issue?

speaker: ALEX
text: Those are more advanced administrative actions. Transfer is for when the issue belongs in another repository. Deletion is rare and usually reserved for spam or mistakes, depending on project policy.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's talk about one special label: good first issue.

speaker: ALEX
text: That label is often your entry point into open source. It signals work that's considered approachable for newcomers.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So if I'm overwhelmed by a huge issues list, good first issue gives me a starting lane.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. It doesn't guarantee trivial work, but it usually means the maintainers think the task is suitable for someone getting started.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now I really want to spend time on accessibility-specific issue writing, because this audience needs that.

speaker: ALEX
text: Absolutely. An accessibility issue should be concrete, reproducible, and respectful. Say what assistive technology or visual setting you used. Describe what happened, what you expected, how someone can reproduce it, the environment, and any additional context.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So not just "screen reader broken."

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Better would be something like: when navigating to the keyboard shortcuts table with NVDA and Firefox, the modifier key is described incorrectly. Expected behavior: the table should identify Insert or Caps Lock as the NVDA key. Reproduction steps: open the page, jump to the shortcuts table, read the NVDA row. Environment: Windows, Firefox, NVDA version such-and-such. Additional context: the incorrect instruction may mislead new users.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That's much more actionable.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. A well-filed accessibility issue respects both the user impact and the maintainer's need for clear evidence.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That leads naturally to writing effective issues in general.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. For bug reports, you usually want a clear problem statement, expected behavior, reproduction steps, environment, and context. For feature requests, explain the need, who benefits, what outcome you want, and any constraints or examples. For all issue types, write specifically, keep the scope clear, and avoid vague titles and vague bodies.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Can you give the before-and-after pattern?

speaker: ALEX
text: Sure. A vague issue might be titled "Accessibility problem" with a body like "This page is hard to use." A clearer issue would say exactly which page, exactly what failed, exactly how to reproduce it, and exactly what should change.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So effective issues lower the cognitive load for the next person.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. They reduce back-and-forth and increase the chance of a useful response.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What about navigating these workflows accessibly in practice? The source had some practical notes.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. For screen reader users, it helps to verify page structure quickly. For example, on a new issue page, the title field should be one of the first major form controls. If you use heading navigation, confirm the first heading matches the task you're doing. On the issues list, moving by heading level can be faster than arrowing through every element. If a template picker appears, tab and enter are often the fastest way to choose Open a blank issue.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And for low-vision users?

speaker: ALEX
text: The New issue button is usually prominent and remains visible even at high zoom. The title field is typically full width and easy to locate. The body field expands as you type. After submitting, verify that your title and description rendered correctly on the issue page.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And for sighted learners?

speaker: ALEX
text: You'll often see the new issue appear near the top of the list with an Open badge. Notice that the issue number is automatically assigned and appears in the URL and page title. That number matters later for cross-references and evidence.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Now let's cover the local Git alternative, because the heading list calls that out.

speaker: ALEX
text: Good catch. Working from your local clone doesn't replace GitHub issues, but it can support your issue workflow. From your clone, you can confirm which repository you're connected to with git remote dash v, then use gh repo view and gh issue commands in the terminal without having to navigate entirely in the browser. This is especially useful if you prefer the command line for reading, listing, and posting comments while keeping the repository context grounded in your local checkout.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So local Git gives you context, and GitHub CLI gives you issue operations.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Let's make sure we cover the command-focused mini tasks clearly: list assigned challenge issues, view a specific issue in the terminal, leave a comment, and create a new issue interactively.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. To list assigned issues, use gh issue list with an assignee filter such as at me. To view a specific issue in the terminal, use gh issue view followed by the issue number. To leave a comment, use the issue comment workflow either interactively through your editor or inline with direct text. To create a new issue interactively, use the issue create workflow so GitHub CLI prompts you for the title and body and, where supported, labels and assignees.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And always verify the result after each command.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Read the output. Confirm the issue number. Confirm that the comment posted. Confirm the repository context.

speaker: JAMIE
text: What are the expected outcomes for this chapter?

speaker: ALEX
text: By the end, you should be able to explain what an issue is, navigate to the issues list, read issue metadata, filter and search issues, open and understand an issue thread, leave a useful comment, create a clear new issue, use an at-mention, understand cross-references, recognize when sub-issues are appropriate, and understand the basics of maintainer triage actions.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And for the workshop specifically, you should have completed the challenge actions and posted evidence.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Your evidence comment should say that Chapter 5 is completed and list the issue numbers for Challenge 2, Challenge 3, and the optional sub-issue if you did it. Then you close your assigned challenge issues.

speaker: JAMIE
text: If someone gets stuck, what's the healthiest next move?

speaker: ALEX
text: Pause and simplify. Confirm you're in the right repository. Read the issue or form before acting. Use GitHub's page structure tools like headings and landmarks, or the CLI help output. Ask a facilitator or classmate for orientation help, especially if the interface differs from the lesson. And remember: if a repository uses templates, that's a support feature, not an obstacle.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That feels important. Getting stuck in issues often isn't about understanding the concept. It's about orientation.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Was there a broader learning moment in this chapter?

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Today, you are doing triage manually. That matters because later you may use automation or AI agents for issue tracking, prioritization, or first-response workflows. But if you don't know how to read, file, comment on, label, and understand issues yourself, you won't be able to judge whether an automated system is helping or making mistakes.

speaker: JAMIE
text: So manual issue work is the foundation. Automation is the amplifier.

speaker: ALEX
text: Perfectly said.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And the learning pattern here really is that same claim, act, confirm loop.

speaker: ALEX
text: Yes. Claim the task and context. Act by filing, commenting, or organizing. Confirm by checking the output, issue number, visible metadata, or posted comment.

speaker: JAMIE
text: That pattern is small, but it scales.

speaker: ALEX
text: It does. It works for web workflows, terminal workflows, and accessible workflows.

speaker: JAMIE
text: Before we wrap, I want to circle back to one simple but powerful idea: issues are not just for reporting errors.

speaker: ALEX
text: Right. They're for bugs, features, chores, documentation gaps, accessibility problems, planning, decomposition into sub-issues, and collaboration. If a repository has work to discuss and track, issues are often where that happens.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And if you're new to open source, the issues list is often the map.

speaker: ALEX
text: Exactly. Especially when you use filters like good first issue and read carefully before jumping in.

speaker: ALEX
text: So your practical next step is simple: go complete the issue challenges. File your first clear issue. Read someone else's issue carefully. Leave a helpful comment with an at-mention. If your repository supports it, try adding a sub-issue. Then post your evidence and verify that everything appears as expected.

speaker: JAMIE
text: And once you can do that confidently, you're ready for the next layer of GitHub collaboration.

speaker: ALEX
text: You are. Nice work.
